splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps agency deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous criminal provisions, including Sentencing Guidelines.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts should apply the rule of lenity or defer to agency interpretations when construing ambiguous criminal statutes or sentencing guidelines. This matters because it determines whether ambiguity in criminal provisions is resolved in favor of defendants or in favor of the government's preferred interpretation.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency deference in criminal contexts, meaning ambiguity should be resolved in the defendant's favor rather than deferring to the agency's anti-defendant reading.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency deference (including Auer/Kisor deference to Sentencing Commission commentary) takes precedence over the rule of lenity, allowing courts to defer to anti-defendant agency interpretations of ambiguous criminal provisions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines commentary (Application Note 1) expanding the definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) to include inchoate offenses like conspiracy is entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to the career-offender guideline, which adds conspiracy and other inchoate offenses to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid authoritative interpretation entitled to deference or instead impermissibly expands the unambiguous text of the guideline. This affects whether defendants with prior conspiracy convictions qualify as career offenders under the Guidelines.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is entitled to controlling weight as a reasonable interpretation of a genuinely ambiguous guideline, and inchoate offenses qualify as 'controlled substance offenses' for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Commentary exceeds guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses, so the commentary impermissibly adds to the guideline rather than interpreting it, and is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief under the First Step Act only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria (reading 'and' conjunctively) or if meeting any one criterion is disqualifying (reading 'and' disjunctively). This directly affects mandatory minimum sentencing for drug offenses.

positions.label: 'And' means conjunctive (all three required)
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' as unambiguously conjunctive.

positions.label: 'And' means disjunctive (any one disqualifies)
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law as a factor supporting compassionate release. Some circuits hold that such changes can never contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons, while others permit courts to consider them. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to resolve this but some circuits have held the Commission exceeded its authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law as one factor among others when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the substance is controlled under state law but not under the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the substance at issue to be federally controlled or whether state-law controlled substances suffice. This affects career-offender and other sentencing enhancements for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: State-controlled substances qualify
positions.summary: Holds that there is no textual basis to graft a federal-law limitation onto the career offender guideline, so offenses involving substances controlled under state law qualify even if not federally scheduled.

positions.label: Must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that a prior conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Guidelines only if the substance is controlled under federal law, not merely state law.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Referenced in Henderson circuit split discussion

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to appeals from orders denying attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment in criminal cases.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case is a civil matter (subject to the 30-day civil appeal deadline) or governed by the 14-day criminal appeal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because Hyde Amendment motions are civil matters ancillary to the criminal case.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies to appeals from Hyde Amendment orders because the motion arises in and is part of the criminal case.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Referenced as minority position in Onamuti

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and a good-faith conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the bankruptcy estate or the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held it belongs to the estate, while other circuits have held it belongs to the debtor, creating a circuit split with significant implications for debtor protections in bankruptcy conversions.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), coupled with the broad scope of § 541(a), compels the conclusion that post-petition appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that the Bankruptcy Code's structure, object, policies, and legislative history establish that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtors rather than the estate.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Referenced in Castleman dissent as contrary position

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release when revoking supervision, or whether revocation sentences must be based solely on the breach of trust and not as punishment for the underlying conduct.
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit allows district courts expressly to punish defendants for violations of supervised release, placing it at the extreme of a circuit split. Other circuits hold that revocation sentences should be based on the breach of the court's trust rather than as punishment for the new criminal conduct. This affects hundreds of supervised release revocations annually.

positions.label: Courts may punish for violations
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release when imposing revocation sentences.

positions.label: Revocation based on breach of trust, not punishment
positions.summary: Holds that revocation of supervised release and resulting sentences should be based on the defendant's breach of the court's trust, not as punishment for the underlying violative conduct.

circuits.name: Third Circuit
circuits.caseName: Referenced in Esteras dissent as contrary position

splits.legalQuestion: Whether duplicate electronic images of child pornography count as distinct images for purposes of the sentencing enhancement under U.S.S.G. § 2G2.2(b)(7)(D).
splits.description: The Fifth Circuit clarified that duplicate electronic images count as distinct images for purposes of the § 2G2.2(b)(7)(D) sentencing enhancement, a question on which circuits have reached different conclusions. This affects the offense level calculation in child pornography cases where the same images appear in multiple copies across devices.

positions.label: Duplicates count as distinct images
positions.summary: Holds that duplicate electronic copies of child pornography images each count as a separate image for purposes of the Guidelines enhancement, increasing the total image count.

positions.label: Duplicates do not count separately
positions.summary: Holds that duplicate copies of the same image should be counted only once for purposes of the Guidelines enhancement.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Referenced as contrary position in Fucito
